source_metadata.title_raw: Robin Paige Donohue -  A-18-232 - December 28, 2018 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 28, 2018

Robin Paige Donohue
Windsor Town Attorney
Meyers Nave
555 Fifth Street, Suite 320
Santa Rosa, California 95401

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-232

Dear Ms. Donohue:

This letter responds to your requests for advice on behalf Windsor Town Councilmembers
Debora Fudge and Mark Millan regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 PPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should
change, you should contact us for additional advice.

The Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A).) Accordingly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any past
conduct, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

(1) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Fudge from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to a proposed residential development project for up to 262 single-family homes given that
her residence is located within approximately 230 feet of a traffic barricade that may be required to
be removed as a condition of the project's approval?

(2) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Millan from taking part in those decisions given
that his residence is located on Vinecrest Road and that the project may include the construction of
a street connecting the project with Vinecrest Road within approximately 316 feet of his residence?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-232
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

(1) & (2) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Fudge and Councilmember Millan from
taking part in decisions relating to the project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those
decisions would have a measurable impact on their respective real property interests in their
residences.

FACTS

You are the Town Attorney for the Town of Windsor and the authorized representative of
Town Councilmembers Debora Fudge and Mark Millan. U.S. Highway 101 ("U.S. 101") bisects
Windsor, and most of the Town's major retail and commercial areas are located nearby U.S. 101.
Windsor was primarily agricultural until its economy and population began to grow rapidly in the
early 1980s as housing development began to flourish in the area. The Town incorporated in 1992.

The Town will soon consider a residential development project proposed by the Windsor-
Jensen Land Company for up to 262 single-family homes (the "Project") on a 63-acre site (the
"Project Site") located on the eastern side of U.S. 101. The Project Site is currently used for
agricultural purposes and devoid of structures. There is additional agricultural land east and
immediately northeast of the Project Site, and existing neighborhoods of single-family homes to its
north, west, and south. The Project Site is comprised of four separate parcels, two of which are
currently located outside of the Town limits, but within the Town's Urban Growth Boundary. As
part of the Project, these parcels would be pre-zoned and annexed into the Town. The Project Site
has two different General Plan Land Use Designations, and the total number of allowable units on
the Project Site under the General Plan ranges from 138 to 324 units.

As part of the Town's review of the Project, a comprehensive traffic circulation study will
be prepared to analyze and address potential traffic issues related to the Project, including issues of
street connectivity, circulation, emergency vehicle access, and emergency evacuation. The study
will evaluate different circulation options for the Project, including whether certain streets should
be extended, new streets constructed, or existing traffic-control devices removed. Among other
things, the circulation study will consider: (1) the potential removal of an existing traffic barricade
on the western side of Jensen Lane at the intersection Jensen Lane and Hembree Lane, and (2) the
potential construction of a street connecting the Project Site with Vinecrest Road.

Hembree Lane runs parallel to U.S. 101 southeast of the Project Site then heads north.
Jensen Lane begins just east of U.S. 101 then heads east, intersects with Hembree Lane
immediately east of the traffic barricade, and resumes heading east and serves as the southern
border of the Project Site. Vinecrest Road begins at its intersection with Hembree Lane, northwest
of the Project Site, then heads east parallel to Jensen Lane, with the Project Site in between
Vinecrest Road and Jensen Lane.

Councilmember Fudge owns her residence, a single-family home located on the south side
of Jensen Lane, approximately 914 feet west of the southwestern corner of the Project Site and
approximately 230 feet west of the traffic barricade. The barricade prevents east or west through
traffic on Jensen Lane beyond Hembree Lane. Because the barricade is located on the west side of
that intersection, the barricade does not prevent traffic heading north or south on Hembree Lane

File No. A-18-232
Page No. 3

from turning onto Jensen Lane east of the barricade, or traffic headed west on Jensen Lane from
turning onto Hembree Lane before reaching the barricade. You note that the Town may or may not
require the removal of the barricade as a condition of the Project's approval to accommodate
additional traffic from Project residents.

Councilmember Millan owns his residence, a single-family home located on the north side
of Vinecrest Road, approximately 1,400 feet from the northeastern edge of the Project Site. As
noted above, Vinecrest Road begins at Hembree Lane and heads east. Vinecrest Road then travels
past Councilmember Millan's residence, heads north, splits into two separate roadways, and comes
to two separate ends. Vinecrest Road is the sole ingress and egress for Councilmember Millan's
residence and the other single-family homes in his neighborhood. Currently, there are no existing
homes or other structures on the southern side of Vinecrest Road, across from Councilmember
Millan's residence, and the view from the front of his residence is primarily comprised of open
space used for agriculture.

As noted above, the traffic circulation study for the Project will consider the potential
construction of a connecting street between the Project Site and Vinecrest Road. Traffic from
Project residents would travel north along the connecting street and then turn left on Vinecrest Road
to reach U.S. 101 and Windsor's major retail and commercial areas. The precise location of the
connecting street and its intersection with Vinecrest Road has yet to be determined; the connecting
street may intersect with Vinecrest Road approximately 316 feet west of Councilmember Millan's
residence, or more than 500 feet west of his residence. You also note that the construction of the
connecting street may be a condition of another nearby proposed residential development project
that the Town is likely to consider before the Project.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or using his or her official position to attempt to influence a governmental decision that
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's
financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections
87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are
identified in Section 87103. With respect to the decisions relating to the Project, the facts presented
indicate that Councilmember Fudge and Councilmember Millan each have the following interests:

• A real property interest in his or her residence because he or she has an ownership interest in
that property worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section
87103.)

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue

File No. A-18-232
Page No. 4

involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability
standard applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and
provides that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Because neither Councilmember Fudge's interests nor Councilmember Millan's interests are
explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the Project, those decisions' effect on each of their
interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility, more than
hypothetical or theoretical.

Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest is material. As
pertinent to the facts provided, a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
real property interest is material if the decision:

(11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property, other than
commercial property containing a business entity where the materiality
standards are analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. Notwithstanding this
prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official
to participate under these circumstances if the Commission determines that
there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable
measurable impact on the official's property.

Thus, The Act would prohibit Councilmember Fudge or Councilmember Millan from taking part in
decisions relating to the Project if it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a
measurable impact on their respective real property interests in their residences.

Councilmember Fudge's Residence

Approximately 230 feet east of Councilmember Fudge's residence on Jensen Lane there is
an existing traffic barricade on the western side of the intersection of Jensen Lane and Hembree
Lane that prevents east or west through traffic on Jensen Lane. Jensen Lane resumes heading east
after the barricade and serves as the southern boundary of the Project Site. If the removal of the
barricade is a condition of the Project's approval, both existing traffic and additional traffic from
Project residents will newly be able to travel east or west on Jensen Lane without circumventing the
barricade, thereby potentially increasing the traffic traveling directly in front of Councilmember
Fudge's residence substantially. Because the barricade may be removed as part of the Project, and
because it is within 500 feet of Councilmember Fudge's property, it is reasonably foreseeable
decisions relating to the Project would have a material financial effect on Councilmember Fudge's
real property interest in her residence. Thus, the Act prohibits Councilmember Fudge from taking
part in those decisions.

File No. A-18-232
Page No. 5

Councilmember Millan's Residence

Councilmember Millan owns his residence located on the north side of Vinecrest Road.
Construction of a street connecting the Project Site to Vinecrest Road will enable residents of the
Project's up to 262 single-family homes to travel north from the Project Site and to turn left on
Vinecrest Road to head west toward U.S. 101 and Windsor's major retail and commercial areas.
Considering that Vinecrest Road is the sole ingress and egress for Councilmember Millan's
residence and his neighbors' residences, project residents use of the connecting street and Vinecrest
Road will increase the traffic experienced by Councilmember Millan and his neighbors when
heading to and from their residences. You note that as part of the Project, a connecting street may
intersect with Vinecrest Road approximately 316 feet west of Councilmemember Millan's residence.
Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable decisions related to the Project would have a measurable
impact on Councilmember Millan's real property interest in his residence if the connecting street
intersects with Vinecrest Road within 500 feet of Councilmember Millan's residence.² Thus, the
Act prohibits Councilmember Millan from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

² If the connecting street does not intersect with Vinecrest Road within 500 feet of Councilmember Millan's
residence, you should seek further advice.

sections.question:
(1) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Fudge from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed residential development project for up to 262 single-family homes given that 
her residence is located within approximately 230 feet of a traffic barricade that may be required to 
be removed as a condition of the project's approval? 
(2) Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Millan from taking part in those decisions given 
that his residence is located on Vinecrest Road and that the project may include the construction of 
a street connecting the project with Vinecrest Road within approximately 316 feet of his residence?
sections.conclusion:
(1) & (2) Yes. The Act prohibits Councilmember Fudge and Councilmember Millan from 
taking part in decisions relating to the project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those 
decisions would have a measurable impact on their respective real property interests in their 
residences.
